gh-w2-f1.answer:
Master if-else decision-making — the foundation of every program that reacts to conditions.

Requirements:
1. Create class IfElsePrograms with main method.
2. Program 1 — Even or Odd: read an int from Scanner, print "Even" or "Odd" using if-else.
3. Program 2 — Nested if — Largest of 3: read three ints (a, b, c), use nested if-else to find and print the largest.
4. Program 3 — Leap Year: read a year (int). A year is leap if divisible by 400, OR divisible by 4 but NOT by 100. Print "Leap Year" or "Not a Leap Year".
5. Program 4 — Positive/Negative/Zero: read a number, classify it.
6. Use clear labels in output (e.g., "Largest: 42", "2024 is a Leap Year").

Key concepts:
• if (condition) { } else if (condition) { } else { }
• Nested if: an if block inside another if block — use when one decision depends on another.
• Leap year rule order matters: check 400 first, then 4 and 100.
• Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
• Logical operators: && (and), || (or) for combining conditions.

Step-by-step for largest of 3:
1. Assume a is largest.
2. If b > a, b is largest.
3. If c > largest so far, c is largest.
4. Print the result.
gh-w2-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Login checks (if password correct), pricing tiers (if quantity > 100), and eligibility rules (if age >= 18) all use if-else. Nested conditions appear in tax calculators, game logic, and approval workflows — interviewers frequently ask leap-year and largest-of-three problems.

Compile & run:
javac IfElsePrograms.java
java IfElsePrograms

Test leap year: 2000 (leap), 1900 (not leap), 2024 (leap), 2023 (not leap).
Test largest: 10, 25, 15 → 25.

Push to java-week-2:
git add IfElsePrograms.java
git commit -m "Add IfElsePrograms.java — if-else and nested conditions"
git push

Common mistake: using = instead of == in conditions — = assigns, == compares.

gh-w2-f2.answer:
Learn switch-case — a clean way to choose one action from many fixed options.

Requirements:
1. Create class SwitchPrograms with main method.
2. Program 1 — Day of Week: read int 1–7, print Monday through Sunday. Use switch with case 1: ... case 7: and default for invalid input.
3. Program 2 — Simple Menu Calculator: display menu (1=Add, 2=Subtract, 3=Multiply, 4=Divide), read choice and two numbers, use switch to compute result.
4. Program 3 — Grade Switch (optional): read char grade A/B/C/D/F, switch to print description (A=Excellent, etc.).
5. Every case that falls through must use break; (except intentional fall-through, which you should avoid as a beginner).
6. Include default: case for invalid menu choice or day number.

Key concepts:
• switch (variable) { case value: statements; break; ... default: statements; }
• Works with byte, short, int, char, String (Java 7+), and enums.
• break exits the switch — without it, execution "falls through" to the next case.
• default is like else — runs when no case matches.
• switch is cleaner than long if-else-if chains when comparing one variable to many constants.

When to use switch vs if-else:
• switch — single variable, many discrete values (menu option, day number).
• if-else — ranges (marks >= 90), complex conditions (a > b && c < d).
gh-w2-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: ATM menus, vending machines, and REST API routers map user choices to actions — switch-case is the classic pattern. Android and backend code use switch on enums and strings constantly.

Compile & run:
javac SwitchPrograms.java
java SwitchPrograms

Test menu: choice 1 with 10 and 5 → 15. Test day: 3 → Wednesday. Test invalid: 9 → error message.

Push to java-week-2:
git add SwitchPrograms.java
git commit -m "Add SwitchPrograms.java — switch-case and menu"
git push

Tip: If you switch on String, values are case-sensitive — "Monday" and "monday" are different.

gh-w2-f3.answer:
Practice all three Java loops — for, while, and do-while — to repeat code efficiently.

Requirements:
1. Create class LoopPrograms with main method.
2. for loop — Sum 1 to 100: declare int sum = 0, loop i from 1 to 100, add each i to sum, print "Sum 1-100: 5050".
3. while loop — Countdown: start at 10, print each number down to 1, then print "Blast off!".
4. do-while loop — Menu repeat: show a small menu, read choice, repeat until user enters 0 to exit. do-while runs the body at least once.
5. Factorial: read n (int), compute n! using a loop (5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120). Handle n = 0 → 1.
6. Optional: print multiplication table of a number (e.g., 7 × 1 = 7 ... 7 × 10 = 70).

Key concepts:
• for (init; condition; update) { body } — best when you know how many times to loop.
• while (condition) { body } — checks condition before each iteration.
• do { body } while (condition); — runs body first, then checks (semicolon after while!).
• Avoid infinite loops — condition must eventually become false.
• sum += i is shorthand for sum = sum + i.

Factorial step-by-step:
1. Read n.
2. Set fact = 1.
3. Loop i from 1 to n, multiply fact *= i.
4. Print result.
gh-w2-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Processing lists of orders, retrying failed network calls, paginating search results — loops are everywhere. Sum and factorial patterns appear in statistics, combinatorics, and coding interviews.

Compile & run:
javac LoopPrograms.java
java LoopPrograms

Verify sum: must be exactly 5050. Factorial of 5: 120. Factorial of 0: 1.

Push to java-week-2:
git add LoopPrograms.java
git commit -m "Add LoopPrograms.java — for, while, do-while"
git push

Common mistake: for (int i = 0; i <= 100; i++) sums 0–100 (5050 still works), but factorial loop starting at 0 breaks — start from 1.

gh-w2-f4.answer:
Build pattern programs — nested loops that print shapes. This trains loop control and row-column thinking.

Requirements:
1. Create class PatternPrograms with main method.
2. Pattern 1 — Right triangle stars (5 rows):
   *
   **
   ***
   ****
   *****
3. Pattern 2 — Inverted triangle (5 rows, stars decreasing).
4. Pattern 3 — Number pyramid (5 rows):
       1
      121
     12321
    1234321
   123454321
5. Pattern 4 — Square of stars (4×4 grid of *).
6. Use nested for loops: outer loop = rows, inner loop = columns/spaces.
7. Use System.out.print for stars/numbers on same line, println after each row.

Key concepts:
• Nested loops: for each row, inner loop prints characters.
• Right triangle row i has i stars — inner loop j from 1 to i.
• Number pyramid: print spaces (n-i), then ascending numbers 1..i, then descending i-1..1.
• Pattern logic is about spotting the relationship between row number and what to print.

Tips:
• Draw the pattern on paper first — label row numbers and count stars/spaces.
• Start with stars only; add number patterns once triangles work.
gh-w2-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Pattern problems build algorithmic thinking used in UI layout (grids), image processing (pixels), and matrix operations. They are classic interview warm-ups for nested loop mastery.

Compile & run:
javac PatternPrograms.java
java PatternPrograms

Output should align visually — adjust spaces if pyramid looks crooked.

Push to java-week-2:
git add PatternPrograms.java
git commit -m "Add PatternPrograms.java — star and number patterns"
git push

Debug tip: If you get one long line of stars, you forgot println at the end of each row.

gh-w2-f5.answer:
Learn methods (functions) — reusable blocks of code that make programs modular and readable.

Requirements:
1. Create class MethodsDemo with main method plus at least 4 custom methods.
2. void greet(String name) — prints "Hello, {name}!".
3. int add(int a, int b) — returns sum.
4. double calculateArea(double radius) — returns area of circle (Math.PI * r * r).
5. boolean isEven(int n) — returns true if n is even.
6. In main, call each method and print results.
7. Optional: method with multiple parameters — printStudent(String name, int roll, double marks).

Key concepts:
• Method signature: accessModifier returnType methodName(parameters) { body }
• void means no return value.
• return typeValue; sends a value back to the caller.
• Parameters are local variables — they exist only inside the method.
• main calls your methods; methods can call other methods too.
• static methods belong to the class — call as MethodsDemo.add(2, 3) or just add(2, 3) from main in the same class.

Step-by-step:
1. Write method signature above main.
2. Implement body.
3. Call from main and print or use the return value.
gh-w2-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Production Java splits logic into small methods — one method per responsibility. Code reviews reject 200-line main methods; methods like calculateTax(), validateEmail() are standard everywhere from Android to Spring Boot.

Compile & run:
javac MethodsDemo.java
java MethodsDemo

Push to java-week-2:
git add MethodsDemo.java
git commit -m "Add MethodsDemo.java — methods and return values"
git push

Rule: If a method returns int, every code path must return an int — missing return causes compile error.

gh-w2-f6.answer:
Build a menu-driven ATM simulation — combines loops, switch, methods, and state (balance).

Requirements:
1. Create class ATMSystem with main method.
2. Start with a fixed balance (e.g., 10000.0).
3. Loop showing menu until user chooses Exit (4 or 0):
   1. Check Balance
   2. Deposit
   3. Withdraw
   4. Exit
4. Use switch on choice.
5. Deposit: read amount, add to balance, confirm.
6. Withdraw: read amount; if amount > balance print "Insufficient funds", else subtract and confirm.
7. Reject negative or zero amounts for deposit/withdraw.
8. Extract logic into methods: showMenu(), deposit(balance, amount), withdraw(balance, amount) if you can.

Key concepts:
• Menu-driven design: while (true) loop + switch + break on exit.
• State variable: balance changes across iterations — pass carefully or use a variable in main.
• Input validation before changing balance.
• double for money; print with printf "%.2f" for two decimals.

Flow:
1. Show menu → read choice.
2. If exit, break loop.
3. Otherwise perform action, show new balance.
4. Repeat.
gh-w2-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Banking apps, payment gateways, and wallet systems all follow menu + balance + transaction patterns. This mini-project demonstrates you can build interactive console apps — a stepping stone to full CRUD applications.

Compile & run:
javac ATMSystem.java
java ATMSystem

Test: deposit 500, withdraw 200, withdraw 50000 (should fail), check balance.

Push to java-week-2:
git add ATMSystem.java
git commit -m "Add ATMSystem.java — menu-driven ATM simulation"
git push

Security note: Real ATMs use databases and authentication — your version is a learning simulation only.

gh-w2-f7.answer:
Student Result system — read marks for multiple subjects, compute total, percentage, pass/fail, and grade.

Requirements:
1. Create class StudentResult with main method.
2. Read student name (String) and marks for 3 subjects (ints or doubles), each 0–100.
3. Validate each mark — if out of range, print error and stop or re-prompt.
4. Calculate total and percentage (total / 3).
5. Pass/Fail: percentage >= 40 → Pass, else Fail.
6. Grade using if-else if:
   • 90+ → A, 80+ → B, 70+ → C, 60+ → D, below 60 → F
7. Print formatted report with name, subject marks, total, percentage, result, grade.

Key concepts:
• Accumulator pattern: total = 0; total += mark1; total += mark2; ...
• Percentage = (sum / maxPossible) * 100 — here max is 300 for 3 subjects.
• Combine validation + calculation + classification in one cohesive program.
• Use printf for clean tables: %-15s for aligned columns.
gh-w2-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: School ERP systems, LMS platforms, and report cards use this exact pipeline: collect scores → aggregate → classify → display. Hiring managers recognize this as a complete small business feature.

Compile & run:
javac StudentResult.java
java StudentResult

Test: marks 85, 90, 88 → Pass, Grade A. Marks 30, 35, 38 → Fail, Grade F.

Push to java-week-2:
git add StudentResult.java
git commit -m "Add StudentResult.java — marks, pass/fail, grade"
git push

gh-w2-f8.answer:
Number Utility toolkit — one program (or menu) with classic number algorithms: prime check, palindrome, reverse.

Requirements:
1. Create class NumberUtility with main method.
2. Menu loop (or separate demos in main):
   1. Check Prime
   2. Check Palindrome
   3. Reverse Number
   4. Exit
3. Prime: read n, print prime if n > 1 and divisible only by 1 and itself. Loop i from 2 to sqrt(n) or n/2 — if n % i == 0, not prime.
4. Palindrome: read n, reverse it, compare with original (121 → palindrome, 123 → not).
5. Reverse: read n, print reversed digits (123 → 321).
6. Implement each as a method: isPrime(int), isPalindrome(int), reverse(int).

Key concepts:
• Prime optimization: check divisors only up to i * i <= n.
• Reverse digits: while n > 0, lastDigit = n % 10, reversed = reversed * 10 + lastDigit, n /= 10.
• Palindrome: compare original with reversed — store original in a temp variable before reversing.
• 0 and 1 are not prime.
gh-w2-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Number algorithms appear in cryptography (primes), data validation (palindromic IDs), and coding interviews. Splitting into isPrime/reverse methods mirrors utility classes in real codebases (MathUtils, StringUtils).

Compile & run:
javac NumberUtility.java
java NumberUtility

Test prime: 17 (yes), 18 (no). Palindrome: 1221 (yes). Reverse: 456 → 654.

Push to java-week-2:
git add NumberUtility.java
git commit -m "Add NumberUtility.java — prime, palindrome, reverse"
git push

gh-w2-f9.answer:
Restaurant Billing system — calculate bill with item prices, tax, and discount.

Requirements:
1. Create class RestaurantBilling with main method.
2. Read number of items (or fixed 3 items for simplicity).
3. For each item: read name (String) and price (double).
4. Compute subtotal = sum of all item prices.
5. Apply discount: if subtotal > 1000 → 10% off; else if subtotal > 500 → 5% off; else 0%.
6. Apply tax: 5% GST on amount after discount.
7. Grand total = discounted subtotal + tax.
8. Print itemized bill: each item, subtotal, discount amount, tax, grand total — use printf for currency.

Key concepts:
• Subtotal → discount → taxable amount → tax → grand total (order matters).
• discountAmount = subtotal * (discountPercent / 100.0)
• tax = (subtotal - discountAmount) * 0.05
• Use loops to read multiple items.
• Format money: printf "Rs. %.2f%n"
gh-w2-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: POS systems, e-commerce checkout, and invoice generators all follow subtotal → discounts → tax → total. This is one of the most common beginner-to-junior interview projects.

Compile & run:
javac RestaurantBilling.java
java RestaurantBilling

Test: 3 items totaling 1200 → 10% discount, then 5% tax on 1080.

Push to java-week-2:
git add RestaurantBilling.java
git commit -m "Add RestaurantBilling.java — bill with tax and discount"
git push

Watch out: apply discount before tax, not the other way around — that's how real billing works.

gh-w2-f10.answer:
README.md is the front page of your java-week-2 repository.

Write a README that includes:
1. Title: # java-week-2
2. Description — Week 2 control flow and methods programs for Prathyu Academy.
3. Topics covered — bullet list: if-else, nested if, switch-case, for/while/do-while loops, patterns, methods, menu-driven apps.
4. How to run — JDK 17+, javac *.java, example runs for ATMSystem and LoopPrograms.
5. File table — all 9 .java files with one-line descriptions.
6. Sample output — paste one small output block (e.g., pattern or ATM menu).
7. What you learned — 3–5 sentences in your own words.
8. Optional: author name and date.

Markdown tips: # headings, - bullets, `backticks` for commands, ```bash for terminal blocks, table with | columns |.
gh-w2-f10.realWorld:
Why it matters: Week 2 repos prove you moved beyond basics into logic and mini-projects. Interviewers skim README first — a file table showing ATMSystem and RestaurantBilling signals you can build interactive programs.

No compile step for README — preview in IDE or on GitHub after push.

Push to java-week-2:

git commit -m "Add README — Week 2 control flow summary"
git push

After pushing, verify headings and code blocks render correctly on github.com.
gh-w2-f10.code:
# java-week-2

Week 2 Java — control flow, loops, methods, and console mini-projects for Prathyu Academy.

## Topics covered
- if-else and nested conditions (leap year, largest of 3)
- switch-case (menu, day of week)
- for, while, do-while loops (sum, factorial)
- Pattern programs (stars, number pyramid)
- Methods with parameters and return values
- Menu-driven ATM simulation
- Student results, number utilities, restaurant billing

## How to run
Requires JDK 17+.

```bash
javac *.java
java ATMSystem
java LoopPrograms
```

## Files
| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| IfElsePrograms.java | if-else, nested if, leap year |
| SwitchPrograms.java | switch-case menu and day names |
| LoopPrograms.java | for/while/do-while, sum, factorial |
| PatternPrograms.java | Star and number pyramids |
| MethodsDemo.java | Methods, parameters, return values |
| ATMSystem.java | Menu-driven ATM simulation |
| StudentResult.java | Marks, pass/fail, grade |
| NumberUtility.java | Prime, palindrome, reverse |
| RestaurantBilling.java | Bill with tax and discount |

## What I learned
This week I learned to control program flow with if-else and switch, repeat actions with loops, and organize code into methods. Building the ATM and billing systems helped me combine everything into real mini-applications.
